segments.text: Hello everyone. Thanks for taking the
time to join this session today. My name
is Manish Kapur and I lead the product
marketing team at Sonar. Today I'm
excited to share with you how Sonar can
help you choose the LLMs that you use
for coding. Over the next 20 minutes,
I'm going to walk you through the
results of the evaluation of over 40
leading LLMs. My LinkedIn and X
coordinates are on the slide if you'd
like to connect and continue the
conversation after this session.
Let's start with the obvious. AI is writing a
lot of code. What we see here is that
the red curve of AI generated code is
skyrocketing while the blue curve for
human written code looks almost flat by
comparison.
Every pull request, every sprint, you
see more code coming out of Copilot,
Cursor, Claude, and any other LLMs that
you're using. The speed is amazing. But
that leads to our next question. Is AI
producing good code or not? Let's be
honest with the answer. Unless you
evaluate the LLMs and verify the code,
you cannot be sure. So that's what we
are going to do today. We'll look at
different LLMs, look at their
characteristics, look at their output,
and understand how they behave.
Let's take a step back. When any
state-of-the-art LLM is released,
typically the vendor will provide you
with some benchmark numbers. These are
industry standard benchmarks. Industry
standard benchmarks are basically
measuring the functional correctness of
the code. That's a good start, but
that's not enough. You do need more
evaluation of models to truly understand
how they're performing. So standard
benchmarks measure functional
correctness. They look at whether the
algorithm can be implemented correctly
or not. They look at pass fail metrics
and things like that. We have standard
benchmarks like HumanEval, MBP. So these
benchmarks are always quoted whenever a
new LLM comes out. But what's missing in
these LLM benchmarks is the real world
assessment of the quality of the code
generated by these LLMs. The LLM
benchmarks do not talk about how secure
the code is or how maintainable the code
is or how much tech debt are you
introducing by using the code as is. So
these are some of the questions that we
wanted to answer. So at Sonar we are all
about code quality and code security.
For the last 17 years, we have been
focused on helping developers and now AI
produce better code. When I say better
code, it's code that is reliable,
maintainable, readable, etc. So, what we
did was we created a framework at Sonar
that can help us evaluate the coding
output of different LLMs. This is a very
comprehensive framework where we have
4,400 plus Java programming assignments
that we give to different LLMs and then
analyze the output of the LLMs using
SonarQube Enterprise. This data will help
you understand how each LLM works and
what kind of issues it produces. So in
our evaluation, we tested about 40 plus
models including Opus 4.6 Thinking, one
of the newer models, and all the way to
Claude Sonnet 4.5 and even the Llama
models and the older GPT models. So we
have a comprehensive leaderboard that
shows the results of all these 40 plus
models that we have evaluated.
Why do the LLMs produce these errors or
why do they produce issues in the code
that they generate? It all comes down to
three broad things. Mixed quality data.
So the code on which they are trained on
is the same code that humans have
written over the last 20 years. So
there's some good code in there, there's
some bad code in there. This code has
some security bugs in it, some hidden
bugs in it. And LLMs are being trained
on that data. As they get trained on
this data, they inherently pick up some
of the characteristics and traits and
the good and the bad from the training
data that they are trained on. So in
addition to that, the LLMs themselves
are probabilistic by nature. What does
that mean? That means they are basically
doing a kind of pattern matching and
predictability of the results. So each
time you might see a different result.
You might see things where the pattern
is wrongly matched or correctly matched.
Right? So there is a chance of error all
the time because of the way they
operate. They also have limited context
and they are hard to explain. So these
are some other factors which lead us to
believe that LLMs will have issues in
the code that they produce. So and
that's not just me telling LLMs have
problems. We have the proof in front of
us. We have LLM leaderboard that we
launched last year. You can access it at
sonar.com/leaderboard.
It's a collection of 40 plus LLMs today
that are ranked by performance. We look
at not just standard benchmark results,
the functional performance of the
benchmarks, but we go deeper. We look at
the qualitative analysis. We look at the
security of the code. We look at the
complexity of the code. So we look at
all attributes of the code when we
measure the performance of these LLMs.
So let's dive deeper into it as to what
we found in our analysis of these 40
plus LLMs. So here on this slide I'm
showing you top five LLMs out of the 10
LLMs that have a greater than 80% pass
rate. On this slide I'm talking about
two things. One is the pass rate for
each of the LLMs. And next we're looking
at the issue density per thousand lines
of code. Issue density and pass rate
give us a good snapshot as to how well
the LLMs are performing. in this list
what we found as of today and of course
in future we'll have more LLMs and we'll
be updating the results as we find more
LLMs, newer LLMs come out, we'll be
updating these results. But as of today
we found that Opus 4.5 Thinking is the
leading model in terms of both the pass
rate as well as the lowest issue
density. The lower the issues, the
better the model is and higher the pass
rate the better the model is. So we have
got it ranked here in terms of both the
pass rate as well as the issue density.
Opus 4.5 followed by 4.6 and then the
Gemini 3 models are the ones which are
leading the list as of today. So in
addition to looking at the pass rates
and the issue density, we also went
ahead and looked at the complexity of
the code. This is something really
important. Complexity tells us how easy
or how hard it is to maintain code base.
So we looked at two parameters. We
looked at cyclomatic complexity and we
looked at cognitive complexity. So what
is cyclomatic complexity? It basically
measures the number of linear
independent code paths that your project
is taking. A higher score for cyclomatic
complexity means it's harder to test and
cover all execution paths of that code.
So we should aim for a lower cyclomatic
complexity in a good codebase. On the
other side we have cognitive complexity.
Cognitive complexity measures how hard
it is for the humans to understand the
code that is being produced by LLMs.
It's all about reasoning. It's all about
understanding the code by human beings.
So what does it tell us? The impact of
this is if the code is harder to read,
review and debug, it becomes harder to
maintain. It becomes complex. It becomes
needlessly difficult and time consuming
to manage and maintain a code base which
has higher complexity. This slide we
talk about two things. We look at the
lines of code produced by each model and
we look at the cognitive complexity and
the cyclomatic complexity for each
model. So what we are trying to do here
is understand how these models behave
and when they generate complex code
versus when they don't generate complex
code. So in general what we have found
is models with higher functional
performance tend to be more verbose and
complex. If you look at our left side
chart in blue it shows you the lines of
code produced by each of those models.
So what this tells us is for a given
programming assignment or for a given
set of programming assignments GPT 5.2
High for example almost took 900
thousand lines of code or a million
lines of code to do the same task where
versus GPT 40 which took less than
200,000 lines of code to do the same
task. So you can see there's a big
variability here in terms of lines of
code produced for the same programming
assignments. What does this tell us? It
basically tells us that a code can be
written in 10 different ways. How many
lines can you use to solve a particular
problem? It depends on the LLM and the
type of training that they have gone
through and the type of reasoning modes
that they have. So on the right-hand
side we looked at cyclomatic and
cognitive complexity of the models. You
can see the red and the blue numbers
show the cyclomatic and cognitive
complexities for some of the models
here. What we can correlate here is for
example going back to GPT 5.2 High, GPT
5.2 High produced about a million lines
of code for doing an assignment and if
you look at the cognitive complexity of
GPT 5.2 and the cyclomatic complexity of
5.2 High, it's quite high. So it's
roughly that the more verbose your code
base is in terms of the code produced by
LLMs, the higher the cognitive and
cyclomatic complexity is and what that
basically means is it'll take you longer
to understand that code, maintain that
code and it's going to be an
accumulation of technical debt if you
cannot maintain that code. So we also
dig a bit deeper into the performance of
the LLMs. So we analyzed the number of
bugs produced by each of the LLMs and we
also looked at the number of security
issues that each of the LLMs produced.
On the left side we are looking at the
bugs per model and you can see that as
and when we have been moving from GPT 40
to Opus 4.5 and 4.6 there's a gradual
slight decrease in the number of bugs
being produced by the LLMs. So generally
speaking, it's not a hard well-defined
trend, but you can see GPT40 had tons of
bugs in it when it was doing a
programming assignment. The same
assignment if it was done by GPT 5.2 or
GPT 5.1 High, the number of bugs were
far lesser. And if you look at the
security issues on the right-hand side,
generally more of all of the models have
started to become very good in terms of
security. They have reduced the number
of security bugs drastically over the
last one and a half years. You can see
GPT4 had a ton of security bugs and now
the newer models like Opus 4.5 and 4.6
and GPT 5.2 High have the least number
of security bugs. In fact GPT 5.2 ranks
as the best in terms of the lowest
number of security issues produced based
on our testing. So what does this data
tell us? This data tells us a few
different things. The current leader is
Opus 4.5 and we are calling it a leader
based on two factors. One is how much
correct code it is producing and what's
the pass rate like? So in this case it's
And what's the issue density like? In
this case the issue density is 15.15 per
thousand lines of code. And this is this
density is the least among all the
models that we have evaluated.
We've also found that after late 2025
and 2026 models have been getting better
and better and their pass rates are
always higher than 80%. Which means the
models are improving in terms of the
output that they're producing.
One other striking fact that we found
was correctness is not equal to quality.
High pass rates do not guarantee that
your model is producing high quality
code. I'll call out Gemini 3 Pro here.
It ranks number three in terms of
correctness, but it produces a lot of
issues. So, it's 24th ranked in terms of
issue density. So, you do need to take
care of looking at the details and
evaluate the correctness of the model,
complexity of the model, the code
quality of the model, the security of
the model when you're evaluating the
models. The other thing which we found
was models which are smaller like Open
Coder 8B which is a relatively small
language model as compared to Llama or
GPT 5.2. Pass rates there are very low
but they are also compromising on
correctness. They generate simple code
but the complexity is less but they're
also compromising on correctness. So you
need to be careful in terms of choosing
your models based on the data that you
see on the leaderboard. It'll help you
guide in terms of which use cases your
models are suitable for. So what do you
do with all of this information that I
shared earlier today? Let me give you
some concrete takeaways. When choosing
an LLM for your team, prioritize models
with 80% or higher pass rates for any
code headed to production. Below that
threshold you are basically spending
time going to spend time fixing than
what you're saving in terms of time.
Look at the issue density along with
correctness. A model with 82% pass rate
and 18 as the issue density count is a
much better choice than one with 81% as
the pass rate and 25 as the issue
density. The review burden is
dramatically different in these two
models.
Favor the late 2025 and 2026 models. The
generational improvement is significant
and measurable in the newer models.
Prioritize readability and
maintainability. Look at complexity of
these models. Your team has to live with
this code. The complexity metrics do
matter. And for your day-to-day
practice, here are some tips. Always
review LLM generated code for code
quality and security. Always. There are
no exceptions. The models are not
security aware and they will make
mistakes. Use automated code review
tools like SonarQube to catch what human
reviews miss. This is exactly the kind
of analysis that scales. Don't assume
simpler is better. Verify correctness.
The simplicity paradox is real. And stay
current. The landscape is evolving fast.
The best model today may not be the best
model in 6 months. Let's wrap with five
key messages. Benchmarks alone don't
tell the full story. You do need to do
qualitative analysis of all the code
that is being produced by LLMs. Newer
models generally are better, but don't
take it for granted. You do need to
evaluate them. You do need to test them.
Verification isn't optional. It's
essential. Quality and security are
non-negotiable. You absolutely need to
make sure that the code being produced
by LLMs is of high quality, secure, and
trustworthy.
Thank you for taking the time today. I
hope you learned something useful today.
Feel free to reach out if you have any
questions or would like to continue the
discussion.
